planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or transplants after last frost in full sun to partial shade in fertile, well-drained soil in zone 9–11; Pepino Dulce (Sweet Cucumber, Pepino Melon) is a sprawling Andean subshrub of the nightshade family (Solanaceae) from the highlands of Peru, Colombia, and Ecuador, producing distinctive oval to pear-shaped fruits with smooth, creamy-yellow to pale green skin streaked with purple, and sweet, juicy, melon-flavored flesh — eaten fresh as a dessert fruit or in fruit salads.

directions: Plant Pepino Dulce in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun; water consistently and feed monthly with balanced fertilizer; stake or cage as plants sprawl; harvest fruits when fully colored and aromatic, giving slightly to gentle pressure. Solanum muricatum is a domesticated Andean crop related to tomato, potato, and eggplant — one of the less commonly grown members of the Solanaceae family that produces a genuinely delicious and distinctive dessert fruit. The oval fruits, ranging from 2–4 inches long, develop beautiful purple streaking on a pale yellow base as they ripen, and the flesh has a mild, sweet flavor combining melon, pear, and cucumber notes with very low acidity — a refreshing warm-season treat eaten fresh, chilled, or in fruit salads. Propagation from stem cuttings is faster and more reliable than from seed, yielding fruiting plants within the same season; plants grown in zone 9–11 behave as short-lived perennials and may be cut back after fruiting for regrowth. In frost-prone zones it is grown as a warm-season annual from transplants started indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost.
sources: Nee, M. — Solanum and its cultivated relatives (Solanaceae review)

planting.note: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost and transplant after all frost danger passes; Strawberry Ground Cherry is a compact, dwarf annual groundcherry — smaller than common Cape Gooseberry (Physalis peruviana) — native to eastern North America and the West Indies, producing abundant small, papery-husked fruits with sweet, tropical-strawberry-vanilla flavor, excellent for fresh eating, jam, and baked goods.

directions: Start Strawberry Ground Cherry seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant to full sun after frost; water regularly; fruits are ready when husks turn tan and papery and fruits drop to the ground — collect daily. Physalis pruinosa is the dwarf cousin of the taller Cape Gooseberry and common Ground Cherry (P. peruviana, P. heterophylla), distinguished by its compact, sprawling habit and intensely sweet, strawberry-vanilla flavored fruits inside papery husks. The plant is more compact than other groundcherries, topping out at 1–2 feet, making it easier to manage in small gardens and containers. Like all groundcherries, the ripe fruits naturally drop from the plant when fully ripe — the papery husk keeps them clean and extends shelf life for weeks at room temperature. The flavor is distinctly different from tomato or tomatillo: sweet, fruity, and aromatic, reminiscent of strawberry jam with vanilla undertones. Used fresh in fruit salads, made into preserves and pie filling, or dried like raisins. Extremely productive and easy to grow, self-seeding reliably in warm gardens once established.
sources: Sullivan, J.R. — Physalis pruinosa, Flora of North America
Whealy, K., Seed Savers Exchange — Physalis varieties

planting.note: Plant two compatible cultivars (cross-pollination required) in spring in full sun in moist, well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in zone 2–7; Haskap (Honeyberry, Blue Honeysuckle) is an exceptionally cold-hardy edible honeysuckle shrub from the boreal forests of Siberia, Japan, and northern North America — one of the first fruits to ripen in spring, producing elongated, dark blue-purple berries with complex blueberry-raspberry-elderberry flavor used fresh, frozen, and in jams.

directions: Plant Haskap in full sun in moist, well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil; always plant two or more compatible cultivars for cross-pollination; water regularly during establishment and fruit development; berries ripen 2–3 weeks before strawberries and should be harvested promptly. Lonicera caerulea var. edulis and its cultivated selections (Honeyberry or Haskap) represent one of the hardiest edible shrubs in cultivation — tolerating temperatures to -40°F or below in adapted varieties, and withstanding late spring frosts during bloom thanks to early-season cold-hardiness of the flowers. Two compatible cultivars are essential for reliable fruiting, as the species is largely self-incompatible; many named Canadian and Japanese breeding program selections are available for pairing. The elongated, dark blue-black berries are nutritionally similar to blueberries but with a distinctly more complex flavor — sweet-tart with blueberry, raspberry, and elderberry undertones and a pleasant tartness. The shrubs are extremely long-lived (50+ years), begin bearing in year 2–3 after planting, and require minimal pruning beyond removing deadwood. Excellent for northern gardens where blueberries struggle, and the extremely early ripening season makes them valuable for extending the fresh-fruit season.
sources: Bors, B. — University of Saskatchewan Haskap Breeding Program

subcategory: East Asian ornamental-edible stone fruit tree

planting.note: Plant grafted specimens in spring in full sun in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in zone 6–9; Japanese Apricot (Chinese Plum, Mei, Ume) is a small to medium flowering stone-fruit tree — among the earliest spring bloomers, flowering January–March before leaf emergence — native to China and widely cultivated in East Asia for ornamental value and for the sour, aromatic green fruits used to produce umeboshi (pickled plums), ume liqueur (umeshu), and traditional medicines.

directions: Plant grafted Japanese Apricot in full sun in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil; prune to open vase shape after fruiting; harvest fruits green-yellow when firm for pickling (umeboshi), or wait for full yellow ripeness for eating fresh or making ume liqueur. Prunus mume is revered in Chinese, Japanese, and Korean culture as the herald of spring — its fragrant, five-petaled flowers in shades of white through deep rose appear January–March, often while snow still falls, making it one of the most poetically celebrated trees in East Asian art and literature. The fruits, called ume (Japanese) or mei (Chinese), resemble apricots but are sharply sour when ripe due to high citric acid content; they are almost always processed rather than eaten raw. Traditional umeboshi preparation involves salt-pickling the green fruits with red shiso leaves to produce a strongly sour, salty Japanese condiment used in rice balls, with noodles, and as a digestive aid — extraordinarily long-lasting, with some umeboshi aged decades or more. Umeshu (ume liqueur, often called plum wine) is made by steeping whole green fruits in shochu spirits with sugar. Many named ornamental cultivars exist with double flowers and deep pink to red color; fruiting varieties for culinary use include 'Nanko' and 'Kairyou Ouseibai'.
sources: Beppu, K. et al. — Prunus mume biology and cultivar diversity, Acta Horticulturae

planting.note: Plant two specimens (cross-pollination improves fruit set) in spring in full sun to partial shade in fertile, well-drained, moist, slightly acidic soil in zone 5–9; Three-leaf Akebia is a vigorous, twining climbing vine from China, Korea, and Japan — semi-evergreen in mild winters — producing fragrant vanilla-scented flowers in spring and remarkable sausage-shaped, purple to pale lavender fruits in autumn, with sweet edible white pulp surrounding black seeds, and occasionally used young shoots as a vegetable in East Asian cuisine.

directions: Plant Akebia trifoliata at the base of a sturdy trellis, pergola, or fence in partial to full sun; plant two or more seedlings of different genetic origin for cross-pollination and reliable fruiting; water regularly; harvest pods in autumn when they split open along the seam. Three-leaf Akebia is a fascinating edible ornamental vine producing unusual fruits that are rarely seen outside Japan and China but are gaining interest among adventurous Western growers. The pale purple to lavender sausage-shaped pods (3–5 inches long) split open in autumn to reveal sweet, translucent white pulp — the pulp is eaten fresh by scooping around the embedded seeds, which are not eaten. The flavor is mildly sweet, subtle, and aromatic — not intensely flavored but pleasantly unique. Beyond the fruit, young spring shoots are blanched and eaten as a vegetable (akebi no tsuru) in Japanese mountain cuisine. The intricate, three-leaflet foliage on twining stems is ornamentally attractive, and the small, dark purple flowers in spring are fragrant with a vanilla scent. A related species, Akebia quinata (Five-leaf Akebia), is very similar but with five leaflets and is widely grown ornamentally — both are vigorous and need substantial support.
sources: Facciola, S., 'Cornucopia II: A Source Book of Edible Plants' (1998)

planting.note: Root pad cuttings in spring in full sun in fast-draining, sandy or rocky, lean soil in zone 5–11; Engelmann's Prickly Pear is a large, native prickly pear cactus of the Chihuahuan and Sonoran deserts of the southwestern US and northern Mexico — one of the most widespread and ecologically important cacti in Texas — producing abundant waxy yellow flowers in spring followed by deep purple-red edible tunas (fruits) in summer, with edible green pads (nopales) harvested year-round.

directions: Root Engelmann's Prickly Pear pad cuttings by allowing the cut surface to callus for 1–2 weeks before placing upright in dry, sandy soil; water sparingly; once established, this cactus thrives on neglect and natural rainfall in zone 5–11. Opuntia engelmannii is the quintessential Texas prickly pear — a large, sprawling native cactus forming dense clumps to 3–6 feet tall with round to oval, blue-green pads armed with clusters of stiff white to yellow spines. In spring the cheerful, waxy yellow flowers (2–3 inches across) open in profusion, attracting native bees and providing critical early-season pollen; by midsummer the flowers give way to deep reddish-purple tunas (prickly pear fruits) 1.5–2.5 inches long and sweet-tart with a distinctive watermelon-like flavor. Both the fruits and the young, spine-free green pads (nopales) are important traditional foods throughout Texas, Mexico, and the Southwest: fruits are eaten fresh, made into juices, jellies, syrups, and candy; nopales are sliced and grilled, sautéed with eggs (nopalitos), or pickled. Handle pads and fruits with tongs — both the large spines and tiny hair-like glochids cause intense skin irritation. Exceptional drought tolerance, native wildlife value, and dual culinary uses make this a natural choice for xeriscape gardens in zone 7–11.
sources: Powell, A.M. & Weedin, J.F., 'Cacti of the Trans-Pecos and Adjacent Areas' (2004)
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Prickly Pear Cactus
